title: Battle of Lusail: Referee Control Breakdown
match: Argentina vs Netherlands — Quarterfinal, Lusail Stadium, 9 Dec 2022
description: In the 88th minute, Argentina's Leandro Paredes fouled Nathan Aké near the Dutch bench, then deliberately kicked the dead ball into the Netherlands substitutes' area, triggering a mass confrontation. Referee Antonio Mateu Lahoz had already issued 12 yellow cards by that point; the match ultimately set a World Cup record of 18 yellow cards (plus one red for Denzel Dumfries) across 48 fouls called. VAR was not used to upgrade the Paredes sanction — discipline incidents of this type fall outside the four VAR-reviewable categories.

relevant_law.excerpt: TODO — exact IFAB 2025/26 wording pending Docling extraction. Covers unsporting behaviour including provocative, contemptuous, or inflammatory gestures; the applicable clause is the deliberate act of provoking a reaction, distinct from the foul itself.

overturn_rate_source: No VAR review was triggered for this incident. Discipline/referee-control incidents are not among the four VAR-reviewable categories (goals, penalties, direct red cards, mistaken identity) unless a missed red-card sanction is involved. Source: ESPN full VAR review log, all 64 matches (espn.com, Dale Johnson, updated 17 Dec 2022).
what_the_model_does_not_know: Referee match reports, FIFA disciplinary committee minutes, or internal VAR audio from the Lusail match
Whether Lahoz received real-time advice from the VAR room during the confrontation
The precise chain of communication or timing that allowed the situation to escalate
Psychological pressure dynamics — crowd, match stakes, accumulated tension — are not features in any current model

title: Argentina vs Netherlands: Penalty-Shootout Gamesmanship
match: Argentina vs Netherlands — Quarterfinal penalty shootout, Lusail Stadium, 9 Dec 2022
description: During the shootout, Argentine goalkeeper Emiliano Martínez used deliberate delay and distraction tactics — feinting handing the ball to Steven Berghuis before dropping it (Berghuis's kick was saved), and prolonged staring before Teun Koopmeiners's kick. The match finished Argentina 4–3 on penalties (2–2 after extra time). The officiating question: at what point should a referee intervene in goalkeeper gamesmanship under the current Laws, and is VAR capable of flagging delay infractions in real time?

relevant_law.excerpt: TODO — exact IFAB 2025/26 wording pending Docling extraction. The goalkeeper may move laterally before the kick but may not engage in deliberate time-wasting or unsporting distraction; IFAB's 2019 amendments clarified feinting rules.

overturn_rate_source: No VAR review occurred. Shootout gamesmanship is not a reviewable VAR category absent a direct red-card offence; Dumfries's red card was issued on-field, after the match was decided, without VAR review. Source: ESPN VAR review log (espn.com, Dec 2022).
what_the_model_does_not_know: The psychological effect of goalkeeper tactics on a kicker's conversion probability
Goalkeeper positioning or movement-timing sensor data during the shootout
Whether gamesmanship materially influenced a specific missed kick versus normal variation
Any post-tournament FIFA guidance to referees on managing goalkeeper delay tactics

title: Griezmann's 98th-Minute Goal Disallowed — Deliberate Play
match: Tunisia vs France — Group D, Education City Stadium, 30 Nov 2022
description: Antoine Griezmann volleyed in what appeared to be an equaliser in second-half stoppage time. Griezmann had been offside from the original pass, but Tunisian defender Montassar Talbi challenged for the loose ball before it fell to him. VAR ruled Talbi's touch was not a 'deliberate play' that would reset the offside phase, and disallowed the goal. A procedural wrinkle: the referee had briefly restarted play before blowing full time, and VAR protocol generally prevents review after a restart — yet the review proceeded. FIFA rejected France's formal appeal on 5 December 2022.

relevant_law.excerpt: TODO — exact IFAB 2025/26 wording pending Docling extraction. A defender's 'deliberate play' — defined as having control over the ball's direction, not merely attempting to play it — resets the offside phase; stretched or uncontrolled deflections do not qualify.

overturn_rate_source: Tournament-wide VAR overturn rate: 25 of 27 total VAR review interventions at Qatar 2022 changed the on-field decision (≈92.6%); 2 reviews were rejected by the referee at the monitor. This is the overall rate across all four reviewable categories — ESPN did not report a separate offside-only overturn percentage. Source: ESPN full match-by-match VAR review log (espn.com, Dale Johnson, updated 17 Dec 2022).
what_the_model_does_not_know: The intent or physical control behind Talbi's header — the core subjective 'deliberate play' judgement
Referee-VAR audio or the specific video frames reviewed at the monitor
The procedural validity question of whether review should have occurred after the apparent restart

title: Opening Match SAOT Offside — Valencia Disallowed in Minute 3
match: Qatar vs Ecuador — Group A, Al Bayt Stadium, 20 Nov 2022
description: Enner Valencia headed Ecuador in front in the third minute — it would have been the fastest goal in World Cup history. The on-field assistant referee did not flag, but Semi-Automated Offside Technology (SAOT) — 12 optical tracking cameras, 29 skeletal points per player — found Ecuador's Michael Estrada fractionally offside one phase earlier. Neither the human assistant referee nor standard TV replay made the infringement visible; only the SAOT 3D animation confirmed it. This was the first major tournament test of SAOT.

relevant_law.excerpt: TODO — exact IFAB 2025/26 wording pending Docling extraction. Offside position is defined by any part of the head, body, or feet (not arms) being nearer the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent.

overturn_rate_source: Tournament-wide VAR overturn rate: 25 of 27 total VAR review interventions at Qatar 2022 changed the on-field decision (≈92.6%). This incident was itself one of those 25 overturns (a VAR reversal of an on-field non-call). Source: ESPN full VAR review log (espn.com, Dec 2022); TNT Sports SAOT explainer (20 Nov 2022).
what_the_model_does_not_know: SAOT skeletal tracking data or sub-centimetre positional measurements
The 3D avatar calculations that flagged the offside position
Whether this level of precision reflects the intent of Law 11 versus its literal application

title: Vinicius Jr's Goal Disallowed for Offside via SAOT
match: Brazil vs Switzerland — Group G, Stadium 974, 28 Nov 2022
description: Vinicius Jr. converted what appeared to be Brazil's second goal in the 64th minute. SAOT flagged an offside in the build-up that no assistant referee had raised and that wasn't visible on broadcast replay — only the 3D animation confirmed it. The goal was disallowed; Brazil won 1–0 via a later Casemiro goal. This was one of several early-tournament incidents establishing the pattern of SAOT overturning calls no human official had flagged in real time.

relevant_law.excerpt: TODO — exact IFAB 2025/26 wording pending Docling extraction. As with other SAOT decisions, the determining question is whether any non-arm body part is fractionally ahead of the second-last defender at the moment of the pass.

overturn_rate_source: Tournament-wide VAR overturn rate: 25 of 27 total VAR review interventions at Qatar 2022 changed the on-field decision (≈92.6%). Source: ESPN full VAR review log (espn.com, Dec 2022); TNT Sports SAOT explainer (20 Nov 2022).
what_the_model_does_not_know: The SAOT positional data underlying this specific call
Sub-frame limb-position measurements — StatsBomb event data records only the outcome, not the measurement
How SAOT's precision threshold changes disallowance frequency relative to pre-SAOT tournaments
